quotes.verbatim_quote:
should first absorb the foundations for patterns, such as scales, modes, simple chords, and four basic
kinds of chord movement: (1) cycle of fifths; (2) chromatic; (3) stepwise; and (4) in minor thirds.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This passage defines the four foundational chord movement types that organize the entire book's progression of exercises.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The authors feel that the practice of patterns has little value unless the student understands
what musical situations befit the pattern. Used in the wrong place or the wrong key, the best patterns
will fail, even in free-form jazz. We have therefore placed accompanying chord or scale symbols
above each pattern. The observation of that symbol while practicing, then, becomes crucial to an
understanding of how the pattern is used.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This establishes the core rule for how every pattern in the book must be practiced — always with awareness of the governing chord or scale symbol.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Most of the patterns contained herein are presented in eighth notes (the rhythmic level of most
jazz improvisation), in a continuing fashion, without rhythmic variation, and without rhythmic
phrase-endings.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the rhythmic baseline for all written patterns and explains why the notation looks metrically uniform throughout the book.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
When the practiced patterns are applied to an impro-
visation, it is expected that the rhythms would be loosened, so that the idea takes on a more lyrical.
natural, and less mechanical feeling.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This gives the key performance rule: learned patterns must be rhythmically freed when used in actual improvisation to avoid sounding mechanical.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
It would be advisable to extend all patterns to the full range of your instrument,
rather than to stop with perhaps only one octave, as it may appear in the written example.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is an explicit practice instruction directing students to go beyond the notated range, maximizing the technical benefit of each exercise.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The metronome markings can be applied as desired. The minimum tempo given should be
achieved before going on to the next pattern, since the patterns increase progressively in difficulty.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This sets the advancement criterion — minimum tempo mastery gates progress to each successive pattern.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In many. instances, it would be helpful to play the patterns very slowly at first, to aid in bearing the
pattern, and then work up to at least the minimum tempo.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This gives the recommended two-phase practice method: slow for aural absorption, then up to tempo.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The instructions for transposition, sometimes even the completion of the pattern in the given
key, must be followed to achieve adequate results. A pattern can be used in any key with any kind
™@ chord, if you can transpose and sometimes adjust that pattern.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This makes transposition a mandatory practice step, not optional, establishing that patterns are only useful when mastered across all keys.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The articulation throughout the book is slurred except where marked, but the articulation
could be changed like the rhythms, if by practicing the pattern different ways it is learned more
completely.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This states the book-wide articulation default (slurred) and gives explicit permission to vary it as a deeper learning strategy.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
By extracting tones No. 1-3-5 from the C Major scale, we arrive at the notes forming the C Major
Triad.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the Major Triad by its scale-degree formula (1-3-5), which is the foundational chord construction method used throughout the book.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Tones No. 1-3-5-6 of the C Major scale form the C Major Sixth Chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the Major Sixth Chord by its scale-degree formula and introduces the numerical chord-building system applied to all subsequent chord types.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Tones #1-3-5-7 form the C Major Seventh Chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This concisely defines the Major Seventh Chord by scale degrees, continuing the additive chord-building system from triad through extended chords.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Tones #1-3-5-7-9 form the C Major Ninth Chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This completes the four foundational Major chord types by adding the ninth, establishing the full chord vocabulary that anchors the book's first section.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This chord tone chart organizes the tones of al! Major scales in the order needed for the construction
of Major chords. Reading up from the chord root C (also indicated as tone #1), we see the tones:
C-E-G-B-D-F-A, which are all the notes in the C Major scale. Note that the ninth, eleventh and

thirteenth are also the second, fourth and sixth tones respectively, as they are the same letter names.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This explains how to read the Chord Tone Chart and clarifies the enharmonic equivalence of extensions (9=2, 11=4, 13=6) — a concept applied in all subsequent chord analysis.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In preparation for the following exercises, be able to recite, write and play the following chords
in every key: Major Triads, Major Sixth Chords, Major Seventh Chords and Major Ninth Chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This sets a prerequisite standard — full-key fluency in all four Major chord types — before the pattern exercises begin.
